lessons.title: Cardiac Root Words and Prefixes
objectives: Identify the most common root words and prefixes related to the heart and blood vessels
Decode cardiovascular terms using word-part analysis
Distinguish between terms describing rate, rhythm, and structural changes of the heart
Recognize cardiovascular word parts in clinical documentation

conceptBlocks.heading: Core Cardiac Roots
conceptBlocks.body:
cardi/o = heart (cardiology, myocardium, cardiomegaly)
myo/cardi/o = heart muscle (myocarditis, myocardial infarction)
coronary = crown-shaped vessels encircling the heart (coronary artery)
arteri/o = artery (arteriosclerosis, arteriectomy)
angi/o / vas/o = vessel (angioplasty, vasoconstriction, vasodilator)
aort/o = aorta (aortic stenosis)
phleb/o / ven/o = vein (phlebitis, venous insufficiency)

conceptBlocks.heading: Rate and Rhythm Prefixes
conceptBlocks.body:
brady- = slow → bradycardia = slow heart rate (< 60 bpm at rest)
tachy- = fast → tachycardia = fast heart rate (> 100 bpm at rest)
arrhythmia = without normal rhythm (a- = without; -rrhythmia = rhythm)
dys- = abnormal → dysrhythmia = abnormal heart rhythm

Clinical note: The terms arrhythmia and dysrhythmia are often used interchangeably; arrhythmia is the more traditional term in cardiology.

conceptBlocks.heading: Structural and Pathological Terms
conceptBlocks.body:
cardio-megaly = enlarged heart
myo-card-itis = inflammation of the heart muscle
peri-card-itis = inflammation of the pericardium (surrounding sac)
endo-card-itis = inflammation of the endocardium (inner lining)
stenosis = narrowing (aortic stenosis, mitral stenosis)
regurgitation = backward flow through an incompetent valve
ischemia = insufficient blood supply (isch = to hold back; -emia = blood condition)

lessons.teachingNotes: Myocardial infarction (MI) is a term students hear early in clinical contexts. Use it as the anchor: myo = muscle, cardial = heart, infarction = area of tissue death from ischemia. This demonstrates how knowing roots leads directly to understanding high-stakes clinical conditions.

lessons.title: Blood, Vessels, and Circulatory Terms
objectives: Recall hematological root words and their meanings
Identify terms related to blood pressure, fluid volume, and vascular conditions
Distinguish between terms describing blood cell abnormalities
Apply hematological and vascular terminology to clinical documentation

conceptBlocks.heading: Blood Cell Terminology
conceptBlocks.body:
hem/o / hemat/o = blood
erythr/o = red (erythrocyte = red blood cell; erythropoiesis = production of RBCs)
leuk/o = white (leukocyte = white blood cell; leukocytosis = elevated WBC count)
thromb/o = clot (thrombocyte = platelet; thrombosis = clot formation)
cyt/o = cell (applies to all cell types)

conceptBlocks.heading: Blood Condition Suffixes: -emia
conceptBlocks.body:
-emia = blood condition
anemia = without normal red cell mass (an- = without)
hyperglycemia = high blood glucose (hyper- + glyc/o = sugar)
hypoglycemia = low blood glucose
septicemia = bacteria in the bloodstream (septic/o = infected)
polycythemia = excess red blood cells (poly- = many; cyt/o = cell)
thrombocytopenia = low platelet count (-penia = deficiency)

conceptBlocks.heading: Vascular and Pressure Terms
conceptBlocks.body:
hypertension = persistently elevated blood pressure (hyper- + tension)
hypotension = abnormally low blood pressure
vasoconstriction = narrowing of a blood vessel (vasc/o + constriction)
vasodilation = widening of a blood vessel
ischemia = inadequate blood supply to a tissue
thrombus = a fixed blood clot; embolus = a clot that has broken free and travels
infarction = tissue death resulting from ischemia (myocardial infarction)

lessons.teachingNotes: Distinguish thrombus vs embolus carefully — thrombus is stationary; embolus moves. Use the analogy of a parked car (thrombus) vs a car driving away (embolus). This concept recurs in DVT, pulmonary embolism, and stroke discussions.

lessons.title: Respiratory Root Words and Prefixes
objectives: Identify the root words for each major structure of the respiratory tract
Decode respiratory terms using word-part analysis
Distinguish between terms describing breathing patterns and respiratory conditions
Recognize respiratory word parts in clinical charting and orders

conceptBlocks.heading: Respiratory Tract Roots
conceptBlocks.body:
nas/o / rhin/o = nose (nasal, rhinitis, rhinorrhea)
pharyngo = pharynx (pharyngitis, nasopharynx)
laryng/o = larynx (laryngitis, laryngoscopy)
tracheo = trachea (tracheotomy, tracheostomy)
bronch/o = bronchus (bronchitis, bronchodilator, bronchoscopy)
alveol/o = alveolus (alveolar, alveolocapillary)
pneum/o / pulmon/o = lung (pneumonia, pulmonary embolism)
pleur/o = pleura (pleuritis, pleural effusion)

conceptBlocks.heading: Breathing Pattern Terms
conceptBlocks.body:
-pnea = breathing (root for all breathing-related terms)
eupnea = normal breathing rate and depth (eu- = good, normal)
apnea = absence of breathing (a- = without)
dyspnea = difficult or painful breathing (dys- = difficult)
tachypnea = rapid breathing (tachy- = fast)
bradypnea = slow breathing (brady- = slow)
hyperpnea = deep breathing (increased tidal volume)
hyperventilation = increased rate AND depth, causing CO2 loss
orthopnea = difficulty breathing when lying flat (ortho- = straight/upright)

conceptBlocks.heading: Key Respiratory Conditions
conceptBlocks.body:
pneumonia = inflammation/infection of the lung parenchyma (pneum/o + -ia)
bronchitis = inflammation of the bronchi
pleuritis / pleurisy = inflammation of the pleural membranes
atelectasis = incomplete lung expansion or collapse (atel- = imperfect; ectasis = expansion)
pneumothorax = air in the pleural space (pneumo = air/lung; thorax = chest)
hemothorax = blood in the pleural space (hemo = blood)
pneumonia vs emphysema: pneumonia = infection/inflammation; emphysema = destruction of alveolar walls

lessons.teachingNotes: The '-pnea' family is high-yield. Students often confuse dyspnea (difficulty breathing, a symptom) with apnea (absent breathing). Distinguish by prefix: dys- = difficult; a- = without. Orthopnea is worth flagging because it appears directly in nursing assessments as a position-dependent symptom.

lessons.title: Reading Cardiovascular and Respiratory Documentation
objectives: Parse multi-component cardiovascular and respiratory terms in clinical notes
Accurately interpret meaning from combined terminology without a dictionary
Identify high-risk terminology that signals emergent conditions
Apply correct terminology when documenting cardiovascular and respiratory findings

conceptBlocks.heading: Decoding Complex Clinical Phrases
conceptBlocks.body:
Practice decoding these phrases term by term:

'Acute myocardial ischemia' → acute (sudden onset); myocardial (heart muscle); ischemia (inadequate blood supply) → sudden onset of inadequate blood supply to the heart muscle

'Bilateral pleural effusions' → bilateral (both sides); pleural (pertaining to the pleura); effusions (fluid accumulation) → fluid in both pleural spaces

'Bronchospasm with expiratory wheezes' → bronchospasm (sudden constriction of bronchial smooth muscle); expiratory (during exhalation); wheezes (high-pitched airway sounds)

conceptBlocks.heading: High-Alert Cardiovascular Terms
conceptBlocks.body:
These terms in documentation should prompt immediate assessment:

• Ventricular fibrillation (V-fib): chaotic, fatal rhythm
• Tachyarrhythmia: fast + abnormal rhythm
• Acute coronary syndrome (ACS): umbrella term for unstable angina, NSTEMI, STEMI
• Cardiopulmonary arrest: cessation of heart and breathing (both systems)
• Hemodynamic instability: inability to maintain adequate blood pressure/circulation

Being able to read these terms quickly is a foundational safety skill.

conceptBlocks.heading: Charting Respiratory Assessment Findings
conceptBlocks.body:
When documenting respiratory findings, use precise terminology:

✓ 'Patient reports dyspnea on exertion with 2-pillow orthopnea'
✗ 'Patient has trouble breathing and can't lie down flat'

Precise terminology communicates to the care team exactly what was assessed and is legally defensible. The first example tells the team: symptomatic on exertion, needs head of bed elevated — information that directs care.

lessons.teachingNotes: Use realistic chart excerpt exercises. Present students with 3–4 sentences from a simulated nursing note containing cardiovascular/respiratory terms and have them decode each term before discussing the clinical picture. This bridges vocabulary to clinical thinking.

questions.stem: A patient is documented as having 'tachycardia.' What does this term indicate?
questions.rationale: Tachy- = fast; cardi/o = heart; -ia = condition. Tachycardia = abnormally fast heart rate (typically > 100 bpm at rest in adults). Bradycardia = slow rate (brady- = slow). Arrhythmia = irregular rhythm. Asystole = no heartbeat (a- = without).

questions.stem: The medical term for inflammation of the inner lining of the heart is:
questions.rationale: Endo- = inner/within; card/o = heart; -itis = inflammation. Endocarditis = inflammation of the endocardium (inner lining). Pericarditis (peri- = around) = inflammation of the pericardium. Myocarditis (myo- = muscle) = inflammation of the heart muscle. Cardiomegaly (-megaly = enlargement) = enlarged heart.
questions.keyConceptTested: prefix endo- vs peri- vs myo-; suffix -itis

questions.stem: A patient's chart documents 'dyspnea on exertion.' This term means the patient experiences:

options.D: Painful breathing only when lying flat

questions.rationale: Dys- = difficult; -pnea = breathing. Dyspnea = difficulty breathing. 'On exertion' specifies this occurs during physical activity. Apnea = absence of breathing. Tachypnea = rapid breathing. Orthopnea = difficulty breathing when lying flat (ortho- = straight, referring to needing to sit upright).

questions.stem: Which term correctly describes a blood clot that has broken free from its origin and is traveling through the bloodstream?
questions.rationale: An embolus is a clot (or other material) that has broken free and travels through the bloodstream, potentially lodging in a distant vessel. A thrombus is a fixed, stationary clot attached to a vessel wall. Infarction = tissue death from blocked blood supply. Ischemia = inadequate blood supply without necessarily complete obstruction.

questions.stem: The suffix '-stenosis' in the term 'aortic stenosis' means:
questions.rationale: -Stenosis means abnormal narrowing of a passage or vessel. Aortic stenosis = narrowing of the aortic valve opening, impeding blood flow. -Itis = inflammation. -Ectasia or dilation implies widening. -Ectomy = surgical removal.

questions.stem: Orthopnea is best defined as:

options.A: Rapid and shallow breathing during sleep
options.B: Difficult breathing that is relieved by sitting upright
options.C: Complete cessation of breathing for more than 10 seconds
options.D: Painful breathing due to rib cage injury

questions.rationale: Ortho- = straight/upright; -pnea = breathing. Orthopnea = difficulty breathing that is relieved when the patient sits or stands upright. It is commonly associated with heart failure, where lying flat increases venous return and worsens pulmonary congestion. Apnea = cessation. Tachypnea = rapid breathing. Pleuritis causes pain on breathing.

questions.stem: A patient is diagnosed with 'pneumothorax.' Using word-part analysis, this condition involves:

options.B: Air in the pleural space surrounding the lung
options.C: Inflammation of the lung tissue from infection
options.D: Collapse of small airways in the lower lobes

questions.rationale: Pneumo- = air or lung; thorax = chest. Pneumothorax = air in the thoracic/pleural space (not inside the lung). This can cause lung collapse. Pleural effusion = fluid in the pleural space. Pneumonia = lung infection/inflammation. Atelectasis = airway/alveolar collapse.

questions.stem: The term 'vasodilation' describes:

options.A: Narrowing of blood vessels to raise blood pressure
options.B: Widening of blood vessels to lower blood pressure
options.D: Formation of a clot within a vessel

questions.rationale: Vas/o = vessel; dilation = widening. Vasodilation = widening of blood vessels, which reduces peripheral resistance and can lower blood pressure. Vasoconstriction = narrowing. Vasculitis / angiitis = inflammation of vessel walls. Thrombosis = clot formation.

questions.stem: Which root word is specifically associated with the bronchi of the respiratory system?
questions.rationale: Bronch/o specifically refers to the bronchi (main air passages branching from the trachea into the lungs). Pneum/o refers to the lung or air. Pleur/o refers to the pleura (membrane lining the lungs and thoracic wall). Pulmon/o also refers to the lung (used in pulmonary).

questions.stem: Thrombocytopenia is characterized by a:
questions.rationale: Thromb/o = clot; cyt/o = cell; -penia = deficiency. Thrombocytes are platelets. Thrombocytopenia = abnormally low platelet count, increasing bleeding risk. Leukocytosis = high WBC count. Anemia = low RBC count or hemoglobin. Hyperproteinemia would be excess plasma proteins.

questions.stem: A nurse reads 'bilateral pleural effusions' in a patient's radiology report. What does this finding indicate?

options.B: Fluid accumulated in the pleural spaces on both sides
options.D: Collapse of lung tissue on both sides

questions.rationale: Pleural effusion = fluid accumulation in the pleural space (between the lung and chest wall). Bilateral = both sides. This is common in heart failure, kidney disease, and liver disease. Air in lungs = pneumothorax. Bilateral lung inflammation = bilateral pneumonia. Collapse = atelectasis.

questions.stem: The combining form 'erythr/o' refers specifically to the color:
questions.rationale: Erythr/o = red. Erythrocyte = red blood cell. Erythropoiesis = production of red blood cells. Leuk/o = white (leukocyte). Cyan/o = blue (cyanosis). Xanth/o = yellow (xanthoma).

questions.stem: What does 'eupnea' mean?
questions.rationale: Eu- = good, normal; -pnea = breathing. Eupnea = normal, unlabored breathing at a normal rate and depth. Dyspnea = difficult breathing. Tachypnea = rapid. Apnea = absence of breathing.

questions.stem: The term 'myocardial infarction' (MI) is best decoded as:

options.A: Inflammation of the outer heart sac
options.B: Area of heart muscle death due to blocked blood supply
options.C: Rapid heart rate caused by electrical instability
options.D: Enlargement of the heart muscle over time

questions.rationale: Myo = muscle; cardial = heart; infarction = area of tissue death (from ischemia/blocked blood supply). An MI = dead heart muscle due to coronary artery obstruction. Pericarditis = outer sac inflammation. Tachyarrhythmia = rapid abnormal rhythm. Cardiomegaly = heart enlargement.

questions.stem: A nurse prepares to administer a 'bronchodilator.' Based on word-part analysis, this medication:

options.A: Reduces inflammation of the bronchial lining
options.B: Widens (opens) the bronchi to improve airflow
options.C: Removes mucus from the bronchi by thinning secretions
options.D: Narrows the bronchi to reduce airway hyperresponsiveness

questions.rationale: Bronch/o = bronchus; dilat/o = to widen; -or = agent that does something. A bronchodilator widens the bronchi/airways, improving airflow and relieving bronchospasm. Anti-inflammatory medications (e.g., corticosteroids) reduce inflammation. Mucolytics thin secretions. Bronchoconstriction = narrowing.

questions.stem: The term 'atelectasis' refers to:

options.A: Air accumulation in the pleural space
options.B: Incomplete expansion or collapse of lung tissue
options.C: Inflammation of the alveoli from infection
options.D: Excessive fluid in the lung interstitium

questions.rationale: Atel- = imperfect/incomplete; -ectasis = expansion. Atelectasis = incomplete expansion or collapse of lung tissue (alveoli that fail to open or have collapsed). Pneumothorax = air in pleural space. Pneumonia = alveolar infection. Pulmonary edema = fluid in the lung interstitium.

questions.stem: Ischemia of the myocardium is defined as:

options.B: Inadequate blood supply to the heart muscle

questions.rationale: Ischemia = inadequate blood supply/oxygenation to a tissue (from Greek 'to hold back blood'). Myocardial ischemia = heart muscle not receiving enough blood. This is the reversible phase before infarction (tissue death). Cardiomegaly = enlargement. Myocarditis = inflammation. Tachyarrhythmia = rapid contractions.

questions.stem: A patient is noted to have 'rhinorrhea.' This term means:
questions.rationale: Rhin/o = nose; -rrhea = flow or discharge. Rhinorrhea = nasal discharge (runny nose). Rhinitis = inflammation of the nasal passages. Epistaxis = nosebleed (epi- = upon; -staxis = dripping). Apnea (nasal) = absence of breathing through the nose.

questions.stem: What does the prefix 'brady-' indicate when applied to a respiratory rate?
questions.rationale: Brady- = slow. Bradypnea = abnormally slow respiratory rate. A normal adult resting rate is 12–20 breaths/min; bradypnea is typically < 12 breaths/min. Apnea = absent breathing. Dyspnea = difficult/painful breathing. Eupnea = normal breathing.
questions.keyConceptTested: prefix brady- applied to respiratory rate

questions.stem: Pulmonary edema is best described as:

options.A: Air trapped in the lung's pleural space
options.B: Fluid accumulation in the lung tissue and alveoli
options.C: Bacterial infection of the lung parenchyma

questions.rationale: Pulmon/o = lung; edema = swelling from fluid accumulation. Pulmonary edema = excess fluid in the lung tissue and alveoli, impairing gas exchange. Common cause: left heart failure (elevated back-pressure floods lungs). Pneumothorax = air in pleural space. Pneumonia = infection. Emphysema = alveolar wall destruction.

questions.stem: The term 'phleb/o' or 'ven/o' in clinical terminology refers to:
questions.rationale: Phleb/o and ven/o both refer to veins. Phlebitis = inflammation of a vein. Venipuncture = puncturing a vein to draw blood. Arteri/o = artery. Capillar/o = capillary. Lymphangi/o = lymphatic vessel.

questions.stem: A patient develops a pulmonary embolism (PE). Which of the following best describes this condition using word-part knowledge?

options.A: Air entering the pleural space of the lungs
options.B: Inflammation of the lung tissue caused by bacteria
options.C: A blood clot that has traveled and lodged in a pulmonary (lung) artery
options.D: Chronic narrowing of the pulmonary airways

questions.rationale: Pulmonary = pertaining to the lungs; embolism = obstruction by an embolus (traveling clot or other material). A PE occurs when a clot (usually from a DVT) breaks free and lodges in a pulmonary artery, blocking blood flow to part of the lung. Pneumothorax = air in pleural space. Pneumonia = bacterial infection. Asthma = airway narrowing.
